summary: I was born in Great Falls, Montana, on January 19, 1913. It was where my father, Lawrence Nelson, worked as a chef. My early years were spent there before moving to southern Montana after my mother passed away.

My grandfather came from Denmark and fought in the Northern army during the American Civil War. The family name was originally Nelsen, but due to a misunderstanding when he enlisted, it became Nelson. Denmark is where our roots began before immigrating to America.
My father was raised in Oregon before moving to Montana. Although he was of Danish extraction, his upbringing in Oregon shaped part of his early life before he settled down as a chef in Great Falls.

My grandfather served in the Northern army during the American Civil War. His enlistment was marked by a name change from Nelsen to Nelson due to a sergeant's misunderstanding. This military service is a proud part of my family's history.

The American Civil War is significant in my family history because my grandfather fought for the Northern army during that time. It was in the 1860s, and his service led to the permanent change in our family name. This event connects me to my Danish heritage and American history.

My mother's parents were born in Luxembourg, and she came to Montana from St. Louis as a young girl. This Luxembourg heritage was part of my mother's German and Luxembourger background, which influenced my extended family in Montana.
My mother came out to Montana from St. Louis, Missouri, as a young girl before marrying my father. St. Louis was part of her journey from her German and Luxembourger roots to settling in Montana.

After my mother died, I was taken to Hysham, Montana, by relatives who were actually my second cousins. Hysham was the county seat of Treasure County, a wild place with cowboys and frequent shootings. I lived on a 160-acre ranch outside town and went to school there until the third grade.

Treasure County, with Hysham as its county seat, was where I lived on a ranch after my mother's death. It was the smallest county in Montana and quite wild in those days, with cowboys and shootings on Saturday nights. My childhood there was full of ranch work and adventures with my cousins.

I went to a small county school in Eden, Montana, after attending school in Hysham. The school had only six students, and I was the only boy among five girls. I was mischievous, often bringing mice to school to scare the girls.

My stepmother was born in Oklahoma and married my father after my mother's death. She was only eleven years older than me and had a tough life, including a child from a previous marriage. She was strict but fair and played a significant role in my upbringing from age nine onward.

Charles Marion Russell was a great friend of my father's and a remarkable man I knew personally. He died around 1926, the year I finished grade school. I remember him bringing me the first fresh pineapple I'd ever seen from Hawaii and sharing stories while making whiskey with my father.

Hawaii is memorable to me because Charles Russell brought back the first fresh pineapple I had ever seen from there. This was long before planes flew regularly, so he brought it by ship. It was a special treat and an unusual experience for me as a child.

Butte, Montana, was the site of a basketball tournament where my high school team placed third. It was part of my athletic experiences during high school at Carroll College. Butte was one of the smaller towns we traveled to for games.

I moved to Helena, Montana, to attend Carroll College, then called Mount St. Charles, for high school and college. It was there I began to appreciate athletics and met influential people like Sid Smith and Bill Jones. Helena was a formative place in my youth during the late 1920s and early 1930s.

Carroll College, known then as Mount St. Charles, was where I attended high school and college in Helena. It was a small school with about eighty-nine college students and forty high schoolers. I played basketball and football there and was influenced by coaches and priests who shaped my life.

In 1930, a new football coach came from Notre Dame University to Carroll College. His name was Bill Jones, a former lineman for the famous 'Four Horsemen' and 'Seven Mules.' He encouraged me to play football and gave me confidence I had never had before.

Bill Jones was my football coach at Carroll College in 1930, coming from Notre Dame where he played as one of the 'Four Horsemen' and 'Seven Mules.' He believed I had the guts to play football despite my size and gave me great confidence. Later, he became the chief judge in Washington, D.C., and we have remained friends over the years.

The 'Four Horsemen of Notre Dame' were legendary football players, and Bill Jones, my coach at Carroll College, was one of them. His experience and reputation inspired me when he encouraged me to play football despite my doubts about my size and ability.

Bill Jones was also part of the 'Seven Mules,' the offensive line that supported the Four Horsemen at Notre Dame. Knowing he was one of the Seven Mules gave me confidence as he coached me in football at Carroll College. His toughness and skill were legendary.

Bill Jones, my former football coach, later became the chief judge of the courts in the District of Columbia. He is the counterpart to Judge Sirica and remains a friend with whom I still talk football. His career in D.C. is a remarkable part of his life after coaching.

Father McCormack was a priest at Carroll College who was very good to me as I grew up. Along with Father Riley, he taught me a lot and supported me during my formative years in Helena. Their guidance helped shape my character and faith.

Father Riley was the principal at Carroll College and one of the priests who had a significant influence on my life. He, along with Father McCormack, supported me during my high school and college years. Their presence was a steadying force as I matured.

Sid Smith was my high school basketball coach and a senior college student who helped me improve my skills. He made me practice dribbling for hours to build my balance, which changed my athletic ability. Sid was tough but kind and later became a very religious man and a dear friend.

The priest who taught me algebra and later became the Bishop of the Spokane Diocese was named Bishop Topel. He questioned my presence in his advanced algebra class because I lacked the necessary background. His role indirectly ended my hopes of attending West Point.

The Spokane Diocese is connected to my story through Bishop Topel, who taught me algebra when he was a priest. His questioning of my math skills was a turning point in my educational path. This diocese represents part of the religious mentorship I received.

I had an appointment to West Point and was determined to go, but my poor background in mathematics, especially algebra, made it impossible. I took courses to prepare but struggled, and a priest's questioning ended my aspirations. West Point was a dream that I couldn't fulfill due to educational gaps.

Father Rooney is mentioned as one of the priests who had a lot to do with me as I grew up, alongside Father McCormack and Father Riley. Their support and guidance were important during my time at Carroll College and helped shape my faith and character.
Albert Einstein is not mentioned in my story, so I have no personal experience or connection with him.
The Butte, Montana Keno games are not part of my personal history or experiences as I have shared them. My connection to Butte is mainly through basketball tournaments during my high school years.
Cal Lewis is not mentioned in my story, so I have no personal recollection or relationship with him.
